title: AI is bringing a redundancy bloodbath
full_text:
Australian employment lawyers are dealing with cases involving white collar workers being replaced by artificial intelligence weekly, as new research shows one in three people now wish they had chosen different careers.
It raises questions over how prepared Australia really is for what Independent Senator David Pocock has called a “freight train” coming to disrupt workplaces.
Employment law specialist Roxanne Hart said it was now “fairly common” for her to take on cases involving office workers made redundant due to AI.
“The biggest impact that I’ve seen is through white collar redundancies,” she said.
“People who are in the copywriting, coding, data analytics (and the) administration realm, because a lot of those duties are now being replicated by AI to some extent.
“It’s fairly common now, whereas two years ago it certainly wasn’t even a thing that we were thinking about. (Now) we’d probably do redundancies every week in this space.” Meanwhile, the Real Jobs of the Future report, compiled by Real Insurance, revealed that while half the nation have not yet felt AI’s impact, 32 per cent would have chosen a different career if they had known how widespread it would become.
About 40 per cent of respondents said they did not feel prepared for major changes, a sign of the anxiety now gripping white collar workers. Mr Pocock said thousands of jobs had already been lost to AI and many more were at risk while the government took a “light touch” to “regulation of a technology that will fundamentally change our lives”.
summary: The rise of artificial intelligence is leading to significant redundancies in white collar jobs, with many workers feeling unprepared for the changes.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Employment lawyers report a surge in cases of white collar redundancies due to AI.
A survey indicates that 32% of Australians would have chosen different careers if they had anticipated AI's impact.
Approximately 40% of respondents feel unprepared for the changes brought by AI.
relevance: This trend highlights the need for actuaries and financial professionals to understand the implications of AI on the job market and workforce dynamics.
